infobox.title: Steam-Golems
infobox.imageCaption: Mark IV Combat Golem, Ironclad Dominion

lead:
**Steam-Golems** are massive mechanical constructs powered by pressurised steam and guided by an [[Aether-Crystals|Aether-Crystal]] navigational core. First engineered in 1672 AE by the [[Ironclad Dominion]]'s legendary **Artisan-General Halvor Brasshand**, they have fundamentally reshaped the military and industrial landscape of [[Aethelgard]].

A Steam-Golem is neither fully mechanical nor fully magical — the aetheric core provides intelligence and coordination while the steam engine provides raw power. This hybrid design has proven extraordinarily robust: modified versions have been operating continuously since the 1690s in industrial settings. The [[Lumina Collective]] has repeatedly attempted to outlaw combat-configured golems, citing their use in civilian massacres during the Verdant Wars, but the [[Ironclad Dominion]] has blocked every such motion.

content.content:
Steam-Golems are constructed around a **pressurised boiler chassis** — a reinforced iron skeleton housing a water reservoir, fuel chamber, and the critical **crystal mounting cradle**. The [[Aether-Crystals|Aether-Crystal]] core is installed in a shielded cavity in the golem's chest, isolated from direct steam contact by a copper buffer matrix.

The crystal provides what engineers call **aetheric motivation**: without it, the golem is an inert metal shell. With it, the golem can follow complex instructions, navigate unfamiliar terrain, identify threats, and perform delicate manipulations that pure mechanical systems cannot replicate.

content.caption: Cross-section schematic of a Mark III Industrial Golem

content.title: Crystal Depletion
content.content: When a Steam-Golem's crystal core depletes below Grade-D threshold, the unit enters an uncontrolled "blind rage" state in which it continues operating but ignores all commands. Emergency shutdown requires physical disconnection of the boiler feed — a dangerous operation requiring at minimum two trained technicians.

Over 250 years of development, Steam-Golems have been produced in numerous variants. The **Mark designation** refers to the generation of engineering, while the **class** designates the intended role.

items.caption: Labour-class Golem at an Ironclad mining operation
Combat-class Golems at the 1920 AE Victory Parade
A Siege-class Golem at the Battle of Ashgate, 1847 AE

content.content:
The deployment of Steam-Golems in civilian contexts has been deeply controversial. The **Ashgate Incident** (1847 AE), in which a battalion of Combat Golems entered blind-rage state and destroyed a market town, killing over 400 civilians, prompted the first serious calls for international regulation.

The [[Lumina Collective]] drafted the **Golem Limitation Accords** (1850 AE), which proposed restricting Combat-class units to fortified military bases. The [[Ironclad Dominion]] refused to ratify the accords, and the [[Sky-Merchants Guild]] abstained, citing concerns about their own defensive golems.
A golem does not hate. It does not fear. It simply does what it is told — and therein lies both its perfection and its horror.
content.author: High Councillor Thessaly Brightmind, address to the Luminary Council, 1848 AE
